title: Ensure that the scheduler file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the scheduler file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the scheduler file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The scheduler file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the kube- scheduler service in the master node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system.
input_row.audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the master node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/scheduler Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the master node. For example, chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/scheduler Impact: None Default Value: By default, scheduler file has permissions of 644. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-scheduler/  97 | P a g e
input_row.impact: None Default Value: By default, scheduler file has permissions of 644.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires verification of the file permissions on the kube-scheduler configuration file on the master node's filesystem (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/scheduler). Kubernetes APIs and kubectl do not expose host-level file permissions or allow querying of file system attributes on nodes. This check requires OS-level access to the node to run commands like stat or ls, which is outside the scope of Kubernetes API or kubectl capabilities.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into the master node where the kube-scheduler is running.
2. Identify the location of the kube-scheduler configuration file (commonly /etc/kubernetes/scheduler, but may vary by installation).
3. Run the following command to check the file permissions:
   ```
   stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/scheduler
   ```
   or
   ```
   ls -l /etc/kubernetes/scheduler
   ```
4. Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive (i.e., no more permissive than -rw-r--r--).
5. Confirm compliance: The file should not be writable by group or others, and only the owner (typically root) should have write permissions.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES 1.6 BENCHMARK V1.0.0 ARCHIVE
